questions.question: Why is it important to see food and drink as more than just nutrition?
options: Because meals are also linked to comfort, culture, identity and social connection, all of which support wellbeing.
Because the calories in food are the only thing that affects a person's health.
Because mealtimes are mainly a chance to get tasks finished quickly.
Because everyone should eat exactly the same balanced diet.

questions.question: Roughly how much fluid does most adults' bodies need each day?
options: Around 1.5 to 2 litres, roughly six to eight glasses.
At least 4 litres a day, whatever the weather.
Around half a litre, roughly two glasses.
Only as much as makes the person feel thirsty.

questions.question: Which drinks count towards a person's daily fluid intake?
options: Nothing you drink counts; only food provides fluid.
Only plain water counts; other drinks do not.
Only cold drinks count, never hot ones.
Water, milk, squash, fruit juice, tea and coffee all count.

questions.question: Which of these is an early sign that someone may be becoming dehydrated?
options: Feeling too full after a large drink.
Pale, plentiful urine and moist lips.
Sleeping well and having plenty of energy.
Dark, strong-smelling urine together with a dry mouth and thirst.

questions.question: What does pale urine usually indicate?
options: That the person is becoming dehydrated.
That the person has eaten too much salt.
That the person is well hydrated.
That the person has a chest infection.

questions.question: In an older person, which change can be one of the first signs of dehydration?
options: Feeling unusually energetic.
Sleeping less than usual.
Craving salty foods.
Sudden confusion, drowsiness or an unexplained fall.

questions.question: Who is likely to be most at risk of becoming dehydrated?
options: People who feel thirsty often.
Young, active adults who drink regularly.
Older people, those with dementia, and those who cannot reach or hold a drink unaided.
Anyone who chooses tea rather than water.

questions.question: Some people deliberately limit how much they drink. What is a common reason for this?
options: They no longer need any fluid once they reach old age.
Drinking less has no effect on the body.
They have been told that fluids are bad for their health.
They worry about needing the toilet often, or about incontinence.

questions.question: When completing a fluid balance chart, what should you record?
options: Only the drinks the person refused.
A rough estimate written up at the end of your shift.
The amount you offered, whether or not it was drunk.
The amount the person actually drank, recorded accurately and at the time.

questions.question: What does the NHS Eatwell Guide show?
options: How much fluid a person should drink each day.
The exact number of calories every adult must eat.
The proportions of the main food groups that make up a healthy diet for most adults.
A list of foods that are banned in care settings.

questions.question: What is the MUST tool used for?
options: Screening adults to identify those who are malnourished or at risk of malnutrition.
Recording how much fluid a person drinks.
Measuring a person's blood pressure.
A safe method for moving and handling people.

questions.question: What does 'fortifying' a person's food mean?
options: Adding extra salt to improve the flavour.
Adding extra calories and protein using everyday foods such as whole milk, cream, cheese or milk powder.
Serving only low-calorie options.
Removing all fat and sugar from their meals.

questions.question: How should a person's recorded food allergy be treated?
options: By offering a small amount to test the reaction.
By guessing which foods are probably safe.
As a mild preference that can be ignored when you are busy.
Very seriously, always following the recorded allergy information and never assuming or guessing.

questions.question: What are 'protected mealtimes'?
options: Times when food is locked away for safety.
Times when only certain people are allowed to eat.
Times when non-urgent tasks pause so staff can focus on helping people eat.
Times when staff eat before the people they support.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of assistive equipment that supports independence at mealtimes?
options: A fluid balance chart.
Adapted cutlery, plate guards, non-slip mats or special cups.
A blood pressure monitor.
A hoist and sling.

questions.question: Good positioning to help someone eat and swallow safely means the person should be:
options: Lying flat on their back.
Sitting upright and comfortable.
Leaning right back in a reclined chair.
Standing up while eating.

questions.question: What does the word 'dysphagia' mean?
options: An allergy to dairy foods.
Difficulty swallowing.
Difficulty breathing.
A loss of appetite.

questions.question: What is 'aspiration', and why does it matter?
options: Food or drink going into the lungs, which can cause chest infections and pneumonia.
A normal part of swallowing that is nothing to worry about.
Feeling very thirsty after exercise.
A method of thickening drinks.

questions.question: Which of these is a warning sign of a swallowing difficulty?
options: Feeling hungry between meals.
A strong, clear speaking voice.
Coughing or choking when eating or drinking, or a wet, gurgly voice.
Eating quickly and finishing every meal.

questions.question: What is the role of a speech and language therapist (SALT) in relation to eating and drinking?
options: To weigh people each week.
To cook meals for people with swallowing difficulties.
To decide a person's religious diet.
To assess swallowing and advise on how to keep the person safe.

questions.question: What does the IDDSI framework describe?
options: Standard levels for how thick a drink or how soft a food should be.
The proportions of the Eatwell Guide.
The daily fluid target for adults.
A screening tool for malnutrition.

questions.question: A person's care plan says they need thickened fluids. What must you give them?
options: A thin drink, because it is quicker to swallow.
Whichever drink is easiest to prepare.
A drink you have decided to thicken yourself.
The exact texture and thickness written in their care plan.

questions.question: Which best describes your role at mealtimes as a care worker?
options: To make sure meals are finished as fast as possible.
To diagnose swallowing problems yourself.
To decide each person's diet and prescribe their supplements.
To offer and encourage food and drink, and observe how much someone eats, drinks and enjoys.

questions.question: Who should decide whether a person needs dietary supplements or a change to their diet?
options: Whoever happens to be nearest at mealtime.
The person's family only.
Nurses, dietitians and speech and language therapists.
The care worker on shift.

questions.question: You notice that someone has eaten almost nothing for two days and has started coughing when they drink. What is the right action?
options: Assume they are just not hungry and move on.
Wait a few more days to see whether things improve on their own.
Report your concerns to a senior colleague or nurse straight away, and record what you have observed.
Change their meals and drinks yourself without telling anyone.

questions.question: If you are worried that someone is not eating or drinking enough, you should keep it to yourself until you are completely certain there is a problem.
options: True
False

questions.question: When helping someone to eat, you should sit down at their level, go at their pace and encourage them to do as much as they can for themselves.
options: True
False

questions.question: Drinks such as tea, coffee, milk and squash all count towards a person's daily fluid intake.
options: True
False

questions.question: Dark, strong-smelling urine is a sign that a person is well hydrated.
options: True
False

questions.question: If someone coughs when drinking, a care worker may decide on their own to thicken all of that person's drinks.
options: True
False

questions.question: Protected mealtimes means non-urgent tasks pause so that staff can focus on helping people to eat.
options: True
False

questions.question: Good positioning, sitting upright, helps a person to swallow safely.
options: True
False

questions.question: My Care Academy e-learning can award the Care Certificate on its own, without your employer observing your competence at work.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following can be signs that someone is becoming dehydrated? (Select all that apply)
options: High energy and a moist mouth
Pale, plentiful urine
A dry mouth and thirst
Sunken eyes and confusion in a more serious case
Dark, strong-smelling urine

questions.question: Which foods have a high water content and can help top up someone's fluids? (Select all that apply)
options: Yoghurt
Soup
Jelly
Dry crackers
Toast

questions.question: Who is likely to be at higher risk of dehydration and need closer support? (Select all that apply)
options: People who cannot reach or hold a drink unaided
People with dementia who may forget to drink
Young, active adults who drink regularly
People who feel thirsty and drink whenever they wish
Older people, whose sense of thirst is weaker

questions.question: Which of these can be signs of malnutrition? (Select all that apply)
options: Tiredness and poor wound healing
A strong appetite with meals always finished
Unplanned weight loss
A steady, healthy weight
Clothes or dentures becoming loose

questions.question: Which of these are warning signs of a swallowing difficulty (dysphagia)? (Select all that apply)
options: A clear, strong voice after drinking
A wet or gurgly voice
Coughing or choking when eating or drinking
Food being held in the mouth
Finishing meals easily with no coughing

questions.question: Which of these help to encourage someone to drink more? (Select all that apply)
options: Removing drinks between meals to avoid spills
Leaving a drink within easy reach
Only offering drinks if the person asks for them
Offering drinks regularly through the day, not only at mealtimes
Offering a favourite drink at a temperature they enjoy

questions.question: Which items are assistive equipment that can support independence and dignity at mealtimes? (Select all that apply)
options: Non-slip mats
A blood pressure monitor
Adapted cutlery
A fluid balance chart
Plate guards

questions.question: Most adults need around 1.5 to ___ litres of fluid a day.
The Eatwell Guide suggests eating at least ___ portions of fruit and vegetables a day.
Difficulty swallowing is known medically as ___.
A ___ balance chart records what a person drinks across the day, and sometimes their output too.
When food or drink goes into the lungs it is called ___, which can cause chest infections and pneumonia.
